title: The Regulatory Ceasefire Is Over
text:
The Regulatory Ceasefire Is Over

Christopher Beddor, Tilly Zhang

Gavekal Dragonomics | 9 Jun 2026

China’s regulatory enforcers are back in action. In late May, stock-market regulators fined and punished the online brokerages Tiger and Futu for their involvement in moving money offshore, and announced a broader crackdown on cross-border securities trading. Already this year, different government agencies have blocked Meta’s acquisition of the artificial-intelligence startup Manus, fined the major internet platforms for alleged irregularities in their food-delivery operations, and announced an antitrust probe into online travel service Trip.com, among other moves.This flurry of activity has revived memories of the infamous “regulatory storm” of 2021, when a late-2020 crackdown on internet platforms broadened to tougher regulation of finance, property, education and other sectors. But after equity markets slumped and growth slowed in 2022, the government adopted a more reassuring tone and focused on stabilizing the economy and restoring private-sector confidence (see After The Regulatory Storm).
